narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and full-time commitment to studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 68% chance that you might not pass it initially, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which falls in the lower half compared to most applicants' stronger mid-to-upper rankings, pushing the model strongly toward expecting a bar exam struggle. Your Year 1 decile, also below the typical level, added to this concern, though with less weight. In contrast, your LSAT score, which stands out above the average, worked in your favor by suggesting good potential for success. Your middle family income bracket played a small role in heightening the risk perception, while your undergraduate GPA, slightly below average, had minimal impact overall, and your full-time status was a slight positive. Compared to typical students who get approved, your mid-range Year 1 performance aligns somewhat with theirs, but your notably lower Year 3 decile sets you apart as a higher-risk profile in the model's eyes. We know this doesn't capture your full story or resilience as a Black woman navigating these challenges. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year grades into higher deciles, as that's the biggest lever here, and perhaps seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources to build on your strengths. Reapply after strengthening those areas—you have the foundation to turn this around. We're rooting for you.
